coredata.dc:description: Cancer treatment often relies on radiotherapy, but its efficacy is hampered by limited precision in distinguishing cancerous cells from healthy ones. Quantum dots (QDs), particularly carbon-based (CQDs) and phosphorus-based (BPQDs), have emerged as promising radiosensitizers to improve radiotherapy outcomes. These non-metallic QDs enhance radiosensitivity through mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, modulation of cellular pathways, and interference with DNA repair processes. Hybrid systems combining QDs with bacteria, chemotherapeutic agents, and nanoparticles have demonstrated synergistic effects, offering enhanced radiosensitization. This review highlights the unique properties of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs, their potential in overcoming radiotherapy limitations, and their future integration into multimodal treatment strategies. Despite their promise, challenges related to biosafety, targeting specificity, and regulatory approval remain, requiring further research and development before clinical application.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Therapeutic and diagnostic applications of phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) in cancer treatment. This schematic outlines the multifunctional role of PCQDs in oncology. PCQDs are integrated into hybrid platforms—such as bacteria-assisted quantum dots and nanoparticle-based constructs—to amplify anticancer effects. In multimodal therapy, PCQDs synergize with chemotherapeutic agents and metallic quantum dots to generate ROS, promoting apoptosis. Beyond therapy, PCQDs also contribute to precision oncology through advanced imaging applications, including MRI and PET, enabling accurate tumor localization and treatment monitoring.

Graphene-decorated silver nanoprisms (pGAgNPs) enhance the radiosensitization of colorectal cancer cells. (A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showing the morphology of pGAgNPs. (B) Particle size distribution of pGAgNPs determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS). (C, D) Hyperspectral imaging of HCT116 cells treated with (C) pAgNPs and (D) pGAgNPs, highlighting improved cellular uptake with pGAgNPs. (E) Clonogenic survival assay showing cell survival fractions following treatment with radiotherapy (RT), pAgNPs + RT, or pGAgNPs + RT. (F) Quantification of 53BP1 immunofluorescence foci in irradiated HCT116 cells, reflecting DNA double-strand break repair dynamics. (G) Biodistribution analysis of silver (Ag) in tumor, blood, and normal tissues 24 h after intravenous injection, measured by ICP-MS. (H) Tumor growth delay curves in mice treated without RT. (I) Kaplan-Meier survival curves of tumor-bearing mice subjected to various treatments. Median survival times were: no treatment (34 days), RT alone (48 days), pGAgNPs (40 days), pAgNPs (28.5 days), and both combination therapies (pGAgNPs + RT and pAgNPs + RT) at 60 days. Combination therapies significantly prolonged survival. Statistical comparisons were made using Student's t-test. ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001. This figure is reproduced under the CC BY license [52].

Image-guided evaluation and therapeutic optimization of Gd@C-dots for enhanced radiotherapy in non-small cell lung cancer. (A, B) Representative TEM images of pPD-Gd@C-dots (A) and CA-Gd@C-dots (B), illustrating their nanostructure morphology (scale bars = 100 nm). (C, D) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of (C) pPD-Gd@C-dots and (D) CA-Gd@C-dots. pPD-Gd@C-dots show absorption peaks at 457, 520, and 570 nm, with an emission peak near 630 nm. CA-Gd@C-dots exhibit an absorption peak at 347 nm and emission at 449 nm. (E) PET imaging of mice at 1, 4, 24, and 48 h following intratumoral injection of 0.89 MBq 64Cu-DOTA-pPD-Gd@C-dots, showing biodistribution and retention. The color scale reflects % injected dose per gram of tissue (%ID/g). (F) Quantitative biodistribution analysis of 64Cu-labeled pPD-Gd@C-dots and CA-Gd@C-dots at the indicated time points after injection. (G) Tumor growth curves demonstrating enhanced tumor suppression in the group receiving both pPD-Gd@C-dots and X-ray irradiation, compared to monotherapies. (H) Body weight monitoring of treated mice, indicating minimal systemic toxicity. This figure is reproduced under the CC BY license [74]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Mechanistic overview of radiosensitization by carbon-based quantum dots in cancer cells. This illustration highlights the radiosensitizing mechanisms of carbon quantum dots (QDs) and phosphorus-based quantum dots (BPQDs) in cancer cells. 1. Induction of DNA damage and repair inhibition: QDs enhance radiation-induced double-strand breaks (DSBs) and suppress the non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway, increasing apoptosis. 2. EGFR pathway disruption: QDs interfere with epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) signaling, impairing key downstream survival cascades such as PI3K/Akt/mTOR and RAS/Raf/MEK/ERK. 3. Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress: QDs compromise mitochondrial integrity and elevate ROS levels, contributing to apoptotic cell death. Collectively, these actions potentiate the impact of radiotherapy by sensitizing tumor cells to ionizing radiation.

Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) enhance the radiosensitivity of human renal cell carcinoma (786-O) cells through inhibition of DNA-PKcs activity. (A) Quantification of Annexin V-positive apoptotic cells. Significant increase observed in the BPQDs + IR group compared to single treatments (∗p < 0.05). (B) Western blot analysis of Cleaved PARP expression following treatment with 20 μg/mL BPQDs, 5 Gy ionizing radiation (IR), or both for 24 h. (C) Representative comet assay images showing DNA damage in different treatment groups. (D) Quantification of γH2AX foci per cell at various time points after IR. Data from three independent experiments (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗∗p < 0.001). (E) Western blot showing levels of phosphorylated DNA-PKcs at Ser2056 in BPQDs-treated or control cells 30 min after 10 Gy IR. (F) Immunofluorescence images illustrating DNA-PKcs Ser2056 foci in cells treated with BPQDs or PBS, 2 h before exposure to 2 Gy IR (scale bar = 10 μm). (G) Percentage of anaphase cells with chromatin bridges and lagging chromosomes. Data from three independent experiments (∗∗∗p < 0.001). (H) Tumor growth response in 786-O xenograft mice treated with PBS, IR, BPQDs, or the combination (BPQDs + IR). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗∗p < 0.001). (I) Tumor tissue samples collected at the end of the experiment for further analysis. This figure is reproduced under the CC BY license [37].
Summary of Studies on Carbon and Phosphorus-Based quantum dots for Enhancing Radiosensitivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer treatment often relies on radiotherapy, but its efficacy is hampered by limited precision in distinguishing cancerous cells from healthy ones. Quantum dots (QDs), particularly carbon-based (CQDs) and phosphorus-based (BPQDs), have emerged as promising radiosensitizers to improve radiotherapy outcomes. These non-metallic QDs enhance radiosensitivity through mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, modulation of cellular pathways, and interference with DNA repair processes. Hybrid systems combining QDs with bacteria, chemotherapeutic agents, and nanoparticles have demonstrated synergistic effects, offering enhanced radiosensitization. This review highlights the unique properties of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs, their potential in overcoming radiotherapy limitations, and their future integration into multimodal treatment strategies. Despite their promise, challenges related to biosafety, targeting specificity, and regulatory approval remain, requiring further research and development before clinical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a major global health issue, with its incidence steadily increasing worldwide [1–4]. Given its high mortality rate and social burden, developing effective treatment strategies is of paramount importance [5–8]. Effective cancer treatment remains a critical focus, and current therapeutic approaches include surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy [9]. Each method has its unique advantages and limitations [9–13]. Surgery is often restricted to localized tumors; chemotherapy is associated with systemic toxicity and resistance; while radiotherapy is widely used for a broad range of cancers due to its non-invasive nature and ability to target deep-seated tumors [14–18]. Among these, radiotherapy stands out as one of the most commonly used and effective modalities for treating various types of cancer [19–22]. However, a major drawback of radiotherapy is its inability to precisely distinguish between cancerous and healthy cells, resulting in collateral damage to surrounding normal tissues [23,24]. This limitation significantly reduces its therapeutic efficacy and highlights the need for strategies to improve its precision [24,25].

To address this issue, radiosensitizers have emerged as a promising solution [26,27]. Radiosensitizers are substances that enhance the effectiveness of radiation by increasing the sensitivity of cancer cells to radiation while minimizing damage to normal tissues [28,29]. These agents include chemical compounds, biological molecules, and nanomaterials. Among them, nanomaterials have attracted significant attention due to their tunable properties and potential for targeted delivery [30,31]. One of the major advancements in nanomaterial-based radiosensitizers is the development of quantum dots (QDs). Unlike conventional nanoparticles, QDs possess size-dependent optical and electronic properties, high photostability, and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) under irradiation [32].

QDs can be broadly categorized into metallic (e.g., cadmium- or lead-based) and non-metallic types [32]. Due to toxicity concerns associated with heavy metals, non-metallic QDs—particularly those based on carbon and phosphorus—have garnered increasing interest [33]. These carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs are emerging as safe and efficient alternatives, exhibiting high biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, ease of surface functionalization, and the ability to enhance radiation-induced oxidative stress in tumor cells [34,35].

Recent scientific advances have significantly accelerated the development of QD-based radiosensitizers. Novel synthetic strategies, including green synthesis and hydrothermal methods, have enabled the production of highly uniform, biocompatible carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs [34,35]. In addition, progress in surface engineering and functionalization has facilitated targeted delivery, improved tumor accumulation, and controlled drug release. These innovations have not only enhanced the biosafety of QDs but also improved their radiosensitizing efficiency in both in vitro and in vivo models [34,36]. Furthermore, cutting-edge imaging and analytical techniques have provided deeper insights into QD–tumor interactions and the underlying molecular mechanisms of radiosensitization [37].

In this review, we explore the role of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs in radiotherapy. We examine their applications in enhancing radiation treatment efficacy and their impact on cellular and molecular pathways. Specifically, we discuss their synthesis, physicochemical properties, mechanisms of radiosensitization, and recent preclinical studies. By focusing on these innovative materials, we aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of how QDs can improve radiotherapy outcomes and address its current limitations.

Radiosensitivity refers to the susceptibility of cells, tissues, or organisms to damage induced by ionizing radiation [38,39]. It plays a critical role in radiotherapy, as enhancing the sensitivity of tumor cells to radiation can improve therapeutic outcomes while minimizing damage to healthy tissues [40,41]. The degree of radiosensitivity varies among different cell types and is influenced by factors such as the cell cycle stage, DNA repair capacity, and microenvironmental conditions, including oxygenation levels [23,42,43]. Several mechanisms contribute to radiosensitization and enhance the efficacy of radiation therapy [44]. Ionizing radiation primarily exerts its cytotoxic effects by inducing DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) [37,45]. Radiosensitizers can exacerbate DNA damage by inhibiting repair pathways such as non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) and homologous recombination (HR), leading to increased cell death [37]. Radiation also interacts with cellular components to generate ROS, which induce oxidative stress and further DNA damage [36,46]. Radiosensitizers often amplify ROS production, exacerbating oxidative damage and impairing cellular repair mechanisms [47,48]. Cells in certain phases of the cell cycle—particularly the G2/M phase—are more susceptible to radiation [49,50]. Radiosensitizers can arrest tumor cells in these radiosensitive phases, thereby enhancing radiation-induced cytotoxicity [51–53]. Tumor hypoxia reduces the effectiveness of radiotherapy, as oxygen is a potent radiosensitizer [54]. Strategies such as hypoxia-activated prodrugs and oxygen-releasing nanoparticles aim to alleviate hypoxia and increase tumor radiosensitivity [55]. Key pathways involved in cell survival, such as PI3K/Akt/mTOR and NF-κB, contribute to radioresistance [56,57]. Radiosensitizers targeting these pathways can inhibit survival signaling and sensitize tumor cells to radiation [56,58]. Radiosensitizers can be broadly categorized based on their mechanisms of action [28,59]. Metal-based nanoparticles—such as gold, silver, and hafnium—enhance radiation dose deposition due to their high atomic number, leading to increased local damage [60,61]. Small molecule radiosensitizers, including cisplatin, nimorazole, and metformin, interfere with DNA repair and oxidative stress responses, enhancing radiation effects [62,63]. Biological modifiers, such as hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) inhibitors and checkpoint kinase (CHK) inhibitors, influence cellular pathways to reduce radioresistance [64,65]. Polymeric and liposomal formulations are also designed to improve the bioavailability and tumor targeting of radiosensitizing agents [66,67]. QDs have emerged as promising candidates for radiosensitization due to their unique physicochemical properties, including high surface area, tunable electronic structure, and potential for functionalization [68,69]. Carbon-based and phosphorus-based QDs offer distinct advantages in biocompatibility and biodegradability compared to traditional metal-based nanoparticles [70,71]. These QDs can enhance radiosensitivity by generating ROS, modulating cellular signaling pathways, and interfering with DNA repair mechanisms [71]. Their potential as radiosensitizers makes them attractive candidates for improving radiotherapy outcomes, warranting further investigation into their specific mechanisms of action and therapeutic applications [40,71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots based on carbon and phosphorus: classification and properties
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots are nanoscale semiconductor particles that exhibit unique optical and electronic properties due to quantum confinement effects [40]. Since their discovery in the 1980s, QDs have been extensively studied for various applications in optoelectronics, sensing, and biomedicine [55,72]. Their exceptional fluorescence properties, high photostability, and tunable emission wavelengths make them highly valuable in fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and cancer therapy [34,73]. In recent years, the use of QDs in radiotherapy has gained considerable attention due to their potential to enhance treatment efficacy while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues [33,40].

QDs can be broadly classified into two categories: metal-based and nonmetal-based [34,51]. While metal-based QDs, such as cadmium or indium-containing nanoparticles, have been widely studied, nonmetal-based QDs, including carbon and phosphorus QDs, have emerged as promising alternatives due to their low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and tunable physicochemical properties [40,74]. CQDs are small carbonaceous nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm [35]. They exhibit excellent fluorescence, high water solubility, and low cytotoxicity, making them suitable for biomedical applications [35,74]. Their photoluminescence properties can be tuned by modifying surface functional groups or doping with heteroatoms [33,74]. Additionally, CQDs possess antioxidant properties, which may be beneficial in reducing oxidative stress induced by radiotherapy [74]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a subclass of CQDs derived from graphene sheets [73]. They offer superior electronic conductivity, strong photoluminescence, and remarkable stability [40]. Their high surface area and functionalization potential make them excellent candidates for targeted drug delivery and imaging [40,51]. GQDs also exhibit strong radiation absorption, which can enhance radiosensitization in radiotherapy applications [51]. Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) are nanoscale derivatives of black phosphorus, a layered allotrope of phosphorus with remarkable electronic and optical properties [34]. BPQDs are highly biocompatible and exhibit strong near-infrared (NIR) absorption, making them useful for photothermal therapy and radiotherapy enhancement [34,37]. Additionally, their degradability into nontoxic phosphate ions makes them attractive for biomedical applications [37].

Carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs have demonstrated potential in radiotherapy through various mechanisms [34,35,73]. Their intrinsic fluorescence enables real-time bioimaging, which aids in treatment monitoring [35]. Moreover, these QDs can be incorporated into hybrid or multi-hybrid systems to enhance radiosensitization, improve drug delivery, and enable synergistic therapies, such as photothermal and photodynamic therapy [34]. The subsequent sections will explore these applications in more details, exploring their role in enhancing the effectiveness of radiotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative advantages of carbon- and phosphorus-based quantum dots over other nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of radiosensitization, a wide range of materials, including metal-based nanoparticles (e.g., gold, silver, hafnium oxide), hybrid structures, and inorganic nanoparticles, have been extensively investigated [33,37]. While these materials have shown promising results, carbon-based and phosphorus-based QDs present unique advantages that make them highly attractive alternatives for clinical translation [74].

Unlike heavy metal nanoparticles, which often suffer from long-term biopersistence and potential cytotoxicity, carbon and phosphorus QDs exhibit superior biocompatibility and biodegradability [72]. Carbon QDs, such as GQDs and CQDs, are derived from naturally abundant, non-toxic elements and demonstrate excellent aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence, and minimal toxicity, making them safer for biomedical use [52,72]. Similarly, BPQDs degrade into harmless phosphate ions under physiological conditions, significantly reducing the risk of long-term accumulation in the body [52]. From a therapeutic standpoint, carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs offer multifunctionality beyond radiosensitization. Their intrinsic fluorescence properties enable real-time imaging, while their large surface area and ease of functionalization facilitate combination therapies, including photothermal, photodynamic, and chemotherapeutic applications. In contrast, traditional metal-based radiosensitizers are primarily limited to enhancing radiation dose deposition, with less versatility for multimodal treatment strategies [33,34].

Furthermore, while high-Z nanoparticles such as gold or hafnium oxide primarily rely on physical mechanisms (e.g., the photoelectric effect) to amplify radiation effects, carbon and phosphorus QDs operate through multiple biological pathways. These include enhancing ROS generation, modulating apoptotic signaling, and interfering with DNA repair mechanisms. This multifaceted radiosensitization approach may lead to more effective tumor cell eradication, while enabling lower radiation doses and minimizing off-target toxicity [22].

In summary, the combination of high biocompatibility, biodegradability, multifunctionality, and biologically active radiosensitization mechanisms positions carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs as next-generation radiosensitizers with strong potential to overcome the limitations of conventional inorganic and metal-based nanoparticles.

The integration of QDs with diverse therapeutic agents has led to the development of hybrid and multimodal systems that significantly enhance the outcomes of radiotherapy. These systems combine the unique physicochemical properties of QDs—such as ROS generation and photothermal effects—with complementary functionalities of biological, chemotherapeutic, or nanomaterial components. The resulting synergy enables targeted delivery, amplified tumor damage, and reduced side effects, paving the way for next-generation radiotherapeutic platforms [33,34].

The study by Pengchao Ji et al. explored the fusion of BPQDs with Escherichia coli to create a bioengineered hybrid system aimed at addressing hypoxic tumors— a major challenge in radiotherapy [34]. The bacterial-E (BE) system exploited the natural tumor-targeting capability of E. coli alongside the radiosensitizing and photothermal properties of BPQDs [34].

BPQDs were synthesized via liquid exfoliation, producing nanostructures with high surface reactivity [34]. These were then electrostatically conjugated to surface-modified E. coli. In hypoxic environments, the BPQDs generated ROS under X-ray exposure and exhibited photothermal effects under NIR light, enabling dual-modality therapy. Ji et al. noted that the system not only enhanced RT outcomes but also reduced radiation doses required for significant tumor suppression [34].

One remarkable finding was the targeted accumulation of the BE system in hypoxic tumor regions, while sparing healthy tissues and minimizing off-target effects [34]. This selectivity was achieved due to the bacteria's natural affinity for hypoxic niches, which allowed the hybrid system to bypass normal tissues. However, the study also highlighted the challenges of this approach, including challenges associated with bacterial persistence and immune responses. These risks were mitigated by incorporating mechanisms such as thermal inactivation of bacteria post-treatment. Despite these hurdles, the BE system offers a promising avenue for treating hypoxic tumors while addressing the dose-limiting toxicity associated with conventional radiotherapy [34].

Incorporating chemotherapy with quantum dots provides a powerful strategy for synergistic cancer treatment [33]. Sonia Iranpour's study presented a novel hybrid system integrating GQDs with the chemotherapy drug doxorubicin (DOX), encapsulated in a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) [33]. This hybrid, Apt-PEG-DOX/ZIF-8@GQD, was further modified with PEG and epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) aptamers to ensure targeted delivery.

The study revealed that this system achieved high drug loading efficiency and selective tumor accumulation in colorectal cancer (CRC) models [33]. Under RT, the GQDs enhanced the generation of ROS, amplifying DNA damage and apoptosis in cancer cells. Interestingly, Iranpour emphasized that the pH-sensitive ZIF-8 structure enabled controlled drug release in acidic tumor microenvironments, ensuring that DOX was effectively delivered to cancerous cells while minimizing systemic toxicity [33].

Challenges associated with this system were noted, such as the complexity of synthesis and the need to precisely control particle size and surface charge for optimal functionality [33]. Moreover, reliance on pH-sensitive release mechanisms introduces risks of premature drug leakage in less acidic conditions. Despite these challenges, the hybrid system demonstrated significantly improved therapeutic outcomes compared to RT or chemotherapy alone, highlighting its potential for multimodal cancer treatment [33].

Habiba et al. explored a cutting-edge hybrid system combining PEGylated graphene quantum dots (pGQDs) with silver nanoprisms (AgNPs), yielding a composite termed pGAgNPs [52]. This system harnessed the plasmonic properties of silver to enhance radiation dose deposition while leveraging the ROS-generating capabilities of pGQDs to maximize radiosensitization [52].

In vitro studies demonstrated that pGAgNPs achieved superior cellular uptake in both radiation-sensitive (HCT116) and radiation-resistant (HT29) colorectal cancer cells [52]. The hybrid system preserved the anisotropic shape of silver nanoprisms, which is critical for maximizing plasmonic resonance and ROS production. As a result, pGAgNPs induced persistent DNA double-strand breaks post-irradiation, as evidenced by γ-H2AX foci analysis. Habiba et al. reported a dose enhancement factor (DEF10) of 1.2 and 1.28 in HCT116 and HT29 cells, respectively, surpassing the performance of PEGylated AgNPs alone [52].

In vivo experiments further validated the therapeutic potential of pGAgNPs, with treated mice exhibiting prolonged survival and reduced tumor growth [52]. While the system showed no significant differences in tumor biodistribution compared to AgNPs, the enhanced radiosensitization effects were attributed to the higher oxidative stress induced by the hybrid's synergistic design. Additionally, the authors highlighted the non-invasive nature of pGAgNP administration as a critical advantage over more invasive nanoparticle systems like hafnium oxide [52].

These hybrid systems demonstrate the transformative potential of quantum dots in radiotherapy [52]. By pairing QDs with bacteria, chemotherapeutic agents, or nanoparticles, these platforms offer innovative approaches to overcoming the limitations of conventional RT, particularly in targeting hypoxic tumors, reducing systemic toxicity, and amplifying radiation-induced DNA damage. Further research is essential to address the biosafety and scalability challenges associated with these promising technologies (Fig. 2 ) [52].

Recent advances in nanotechnology have enabled the design of multifunctional nanoplatforms that integrate various therapeutic modalities to overcome the complexity and adaptability of malignant pathological conditions [75–77]. For instance, copper (Cu) and chlorine (Cl) co-doped carbon dots (CuCl-CDs) have been engineered to possess ROS-generating abilities upon laser irradiation, peroxidase-mimic activity for chemodynamic therapy (CDT), and glucose oxidase-mediated starvation therapy. These nanocomposites, when integrated with polydopamine and PEG (forming GCP nanocomposites), not only demonstrated high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 24.4 %) but also synergistically triggered oxidative stress and glucose depletion to inhibit tumor cell viability by 90 % at low concentrations [78]. Similarly, copper, nitrogen, and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, N, S-CQDs) functionalized with glucose oxidase and camptothecin (CuCGC nanocatalysts) exhibited a potent multimodal therapeutic profile. These nanocatalysts facilitated CDT, gas therapy (GT), starvation therapy (ST), and ROS-responsive chemotherapeutic release, resulting in significant tumor cell damage in vitro. The rational design of such nanomaterials—integrating ROS production, enzyme-like activity, and stimulus-responsive drug release—offers a promising strategy for enhanced cancer therapy through synergistic mechanisms [79].

In recent years, the incorporation of QDs into multimodal systems, combining QDs with other therapeutic agents, has gained significant attention in enhancing the effectiveness of radiotherapy [40]. This approach leverages the synergistic potential of QDs alongside other agents, such as small molecules or radiation, to induce greater therapeutic effects compared to monotherapy [40]. In the context of cancer treatment, this strategy has shown promise in improving radiosensitization, inducing apoptosis, and modulating oxidative stress, thereby increasing the effectiveness of radiotherapy while minimizing damage to healthy tissues [40].

In the study by Kosar Esgandari, the anticancer potential of combining 17‐allylamino‐17‐demethoxy geldanamycin (17‐AAG) and silver graphene quantum dots (SQD) with X-ray radiation was investigated in breast cancer (MCF-7) cells [40]. The results indicated that the combination of these agents, particularly when used in a triple combination with 2 Gy radiation, led to enhanced cell growth inhibition and apoptosis induction. Notably, the triple combination therapy significantly reduced cell viability, showing superior efficacy compared to any of the single or double treatments [40].

The mechanisms behind these effects were explored through various cellular assays, including apoptosis analysis and gene expression profiling [40]. The study found that treatment with 17‐AAG and SQD combined with radiation led to a significant increase in oxidative and nitrosative stress, as evidenced by elevated levels of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and nitric oxide (NO). Moreover, the Bax/Bcl-2 gene expression ratio was higher in the triple combination treatment, promoting apoptosis. Additionally, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) expression was notably downregulated in the combination treatments, suggesting a key role for EGFR inhibition in the observed anticancer effects [40].

This study highlights the synergistic potential of using SQD and 17‐AAG in combination with radiation as a promising strategy for enhancing the efficacy of breast cancer treatments [40]. The findings suggest that this multimodal approach could improve the outcomes of radiotherapy, potentially enabling the use of lower radiation doses while increasing cancer cell death. The enhanced therapeutic effects observed in the triple combination therapy further support the concept of using quantum dots in conjunction with other therapeutic agents for effective cancer radiosensitization [40].

Imaging has emerged as a powerful tool for the early detection and monitoring of diseases, particularly cancer [80,81]. Advances in nanotechnology have enabled the development of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs as promising imaging agents for cancer diagnostics [35,74]. These QDs offer high photostability, tunable optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility, making them suitable for integration into various imaging modalities. Among these, MRI and PET are two widely used techniques in oncology [35,72]. The following sections explore the applications of quantum dots in these imaging methods, emphasizing their role in improving diagnostic accuracy and guiding radiotherapy [35,72].

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a non-invasive imaging technique that provides high-resolution, soft-tissue contrast without ionizing radiation [35]. Traditional gadolinium-based contrast agents are widely used for enhancing MRI signals but raise concerns about toxicity and rapid clearance. To address these limitations, Fengyi Du developed a theranostic nanoplatform utilizing gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-doped CDs), which serve as an alternative MRI contrast agent with enhanced biocompatibility [35].

The study demonstrated that Gd-doped CDs (∼18 nm in size) exhibit excellent dispersibility in water and a high longitudinal relaxivity (r1 = 6.45 mM−1s−1), significantly improving MRI contrast [35]. In vivo imaging of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) models revealed superior tumor localization due to prolonged circulation and passive tumor targeting via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [35]. Additionally, biodistribution analysis showed primary accumulation in the spleen and heart, with efficient renal clearance, minimizing long-term toxicity concerns. These findings highlight the potential of Gd-doped CDs as dual-functional agents, enhancing both MRI contrast and radiosensitization, paving the way for precise MRI-guided radiotherapy [35].

Positron Emission Tomography (PET) is a powerful technique for detecting metabolic activity in tissues using positron-emitting radiotracers [74]. While PET imaging offers deep tissue penetration and high sensitivity, its effectiveness is often constrained by the pharmacokinetics and specificity of radiotracers [72,74]. To overcome these challenges, researchers have explored integrating PET tracers with quantum dots to achieve dual-mode imaging. Carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs have demonstrated promise in PET applications due to their tunable fluorescence, high biocompatibility, and functionalization capabilities [72,74].

A study by Fu-I Tung investigated the application of GQDs functionalized with 2-deoxy-d-glucose (2DG) for osteosarcoma (OS) imaging and treatment [72]. While primarily focused on radiosensitization, the study also highlighted the potential of 2DG-g-GQDs for PET-guided tumor localization. These QDs accumulated selectively in OS tumors due to the increased metabolic uptake of 2DG in cancer cells, as confirmed by in vivo micro-PET imaging. Additionally, fluorescence imaging detected strong signals in metastatic lung sites, demonstrating that 2DG-g-GQDs could serve as dual-functional agents for both radiosensitization and PET-based tumor tracking [72].

Similarly, Xiaofen Ma explored the role of gadolinium-intercalated carbon dots (Gd@C-dots) in PET imaging and radiosensitization for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [74]. The study synthesized two Gd@C-dot formulations: carboxylic acid-functionalized (CA-Gd@C-dots) and amino-functionalized (pPD-Gd@C-dots), both radiolabeled with 64Cu for PET imaging. Results demonstrated that 64Cu-pPD-Gd@C-dots exhibited superior tumor retention (up to 48 h post-injection) compared to CA-Gd@C-dots. Cellular uptake studies showed that nearly 60 % of the administered dose of pPD-Gd@C-dots remained in tumors, whereas CA-Gd@C-dots exhibited only ∼5 % retention. PET imaging confirmed prolonged tumor accumulation, making pPD-Gd@C-dots a promising candidate for radiosensitization while reducing the need for repeated injections (Fig. 3 ) [74].

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is an emerging hybrid imaging technique that combines the high contrast of optical imaging with the spatial resolution of ultrasound. PAI operates by converting absorbed light from pulsed lasers into ultrasonic waves, enabling deep-tissue imaging with minimal scattering. Although direct applications of carbon- or phosphorus-based QDs in PAI for radiotherapy guidance are limited in current literature, their strong near-infrared absorption and photothermal properties—particularly in black phosphorus quantum dots—make them promising candidates for future integration [82]. These properties allow QDs to act as efficient photoacoustic contrast agents, potentially enabling real-time tumor visualization and monitoring of therapeutic responses during radiotherapy. As such, PAI represents a complementary modality with potential for image-guided precision oncology [82,83].

These findings underscore the potential of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs as multifunctional imaging agents [72,74]. Their integration into MRI and PET modalities not only enhances imaging precision but also has the potential to optimize therapeutic outcomes, contributing to the advancement of image-guided radiotherapy [72,74].

In the quest to enhance the efficacy of radiotherapy, QDs have garnered significant attention due to their unique properties, including their ability to interact with various cellular and molecular pathways [51]. Their role in radiosensitization is of particular interest, as they can influence DNA repair mechanisms, signaling cascades, and mitochondrial function within cancer cells. In this section, we examine the impact of QDs on three critical cellular and molecular pathways: DNA and genetic pathways, the EGFR signaling pathway, and mitochondrial effects. Understanding how QDs modulate these pathways will shed light on their potential to enhance radiotherapy outcomes by disrupting cellular repair processes, altering cancer cell survival, and exacerbating oxidative stress within cells. These insights are vital for harnessing the full potential of QDs in cancer treatment and improving therapeutic strategies (Fig. 4 , Table 1) [51].

The integrity of the genome and the regulation of RNA processes are fundamental to cellular function and overall organismal health [84–88]. As key molecules in heredity and gene regulation, DNA and RNA are common targets for both environmental influences and engineered nanomaterials [16,89–92]. QDs have emerged as promising materials in enhancing the efficacy of radiotherapy due to their unique optical and electronic properties [37,51]. However, as with any novel material, it is crucial to understand their potential impacts on cellular and molecular pathways, particularly regarding mutagenicity and DNA repair mechanisms [93]. In this section, we explore the effects of quantum dots, particularly GQDs and BPQDs, on genetic material and DNA [37,51].

Mutagenicity refers to the ability of substances to cause genetic mutations, which can lead to alterations in the DNA sequence [51,94,95]. These mutations can result in harmful consequences, including cancer, if they affect critical genes. Understanding the mutagenic potential of materials used in medical applications, such as GQDs, is essential for evaluating their safety for therapeutic and diagnostic purposes [51].

Frederico Duarte de Menezes investigated the mutagenicity of GQDs in vitro, evaluating their potential to induce genetic damage [51]. The study found that GQDs, while showing promise in biomedical applications, exhibit mutagenic properties that raise concerns regarding their safety. Using the Salmonella/microsome assay, the research demonstrated that GQDs caused A: T to G:C substitutions, signaling mutagenic activity. This mutation pattern suggests that GQDs may induce genetic damage primarily through oxidative stress, a common mechanism of toxicity in carbon-based nanomaterials. The study also showed a dose-dependent increase in revertants in strains TA102 and TA104, which are sensitive to oxidative DNA damage [51].

These findings indicate that while GQDs have significant potential for enhancing radiotherapy, their mutagenic effects must be carefully considered, particularly in clinical settings where genetic integrity is crucial. Frederico Duarte de Menezes emphasizes the need for further research to evaluate the long-term safety of GQDs in human applications [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-homologous end joining and DNA repair
ce:section.ce:para: Non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) is a primary DNA repair mechanism that fixes double-strand breaks (DSBs) in DNA. This repair pathway is crucial for maintaining genomic stability, but it can be compromised in the presence of quantum dots, which may interfere with the repair process [37].

Yue Lang investigates the role of BPQDs in enhancing the radiosensitivity of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) cells, a cancer type known for its resistance to radiotherapy [37]. The study reveals that BPQDs increase DNA damage in RCC cells by promoting the formation of DSBs. More notably, BPQDs inhibit the NHEJ repair mechanism by interacting with DNA-PKcs, a key protein involved in the repair process [37]. BPQDs prevent the autophosphorylation and activity of DNA-PKcs, thus impairing the NHEJ repair function. This inhibition leads to the accumulation of DNA damage, ultimately inducing apoptosis in cancer cells [37].

In vivo experiments further confirmed the enhanced antitumor effects of BPQDs combined with ionizing radiation (IR) compared to IR treatment alone [37]. Yue Lang emphasizes the potential of BPQDs as effective radiosensitizers, particularly in overcoming radioresistance in RCC, offering a promising strategy to improve radiotherapy outcomes [37].

These studies demonstrate the complex interaction between quantum dots and DNA repair mechanisms, revealing both their potential to enhance therapeutic efficacy and the need for careful consideration of their impact on genetic pathways (Fig. 5 ) [37].

Mitochondria, often referred to as the powerhouse of the cell, play a crucial role in energy metabolism, apoptosis regulation, and ROS production [55,96]. In the context of radiotherapy, mitochondrial integrity is essential, as radiation-induced mitochondrial damage can lead to enhanced cell death through oxidative stress and apoptotic pathways. Quantum dots, particularly GQDs, CQDs, and PQDs, have demonstrated significant potential as radiosensitizers by exacerbating mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress within cancer cells [55].

A study by Jing Ruan highlights the impact of highly oxidized GQDs on mitochondrial function in colorectal carcinoma cells [55]. The research demonstrated that GQDs, in combination with IR, significantly enhanced mitochondrial ROS production, leading to increased apoptosis rates. Flow cytometry results indicated a marked rise in apoptosis in SW620 and HCT116 cells when treated with both GQDs and gamma radiation, compared to radiation or GQDs alone. This synergistic effect was attributed to excessive ROS generation, which disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm), leading to the opening of mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) and subsequent cytochrome c release. Additionally, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed extensive mitochondrial swelling, cristae disruption, and chromatin condensation in cells treated with GQDs and radiation, confirming severe mitochondrial damage as a central apoptotic mechanism [55].

Furthermore, the oxidative stress induced by the GQDs was confirmed through singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) assays, which demonstrated a substantial increase in ROS levels within mitochondria upon exposure to gamma irradiation [55]. The study also observed elevated phosphorylation of the histone variant H2AX (γ-H2AX), indicative of increased DNA double-strand breaks, further supporting the role of mitochondrial oxidative stress in radiosensitization. Collectively, these findings suggest that GQDs act as effective nano-radiosensitizers by amplifying mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, thereby enhancing tumor cell apoptosis under radiotherapy conditions [55].

The EGFR is a transmembrane protein that plays a crucial role in cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival [40]. EGFR activation occurs upon binding with its ligands, leading to downstream signaling cascades such as the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT pathways, which are vital for cancer progression. Overexpression or hyperactivation of EGFR is commonly associated with various cancers, making it a key target for therapeutic interventions [40].

According to Kosar Esgandari, the combination of 17-AAG, SQD, and radiation significantly reduced EGFR expression in breast cancer (MCF-7) cells [40]. Western blot analysis revealed that while monotherapies had minimal effects on EGFR levels, both double and triple treatments substantially downregulated its expression [40]. The study suggests that SQD, in synergy with 17-AAG and radiation, enhances radiosensitization by disrupting EGFR-mediated signaling, thereby impairing cancer cell survival. Additionally, the reduction in EGFR expression correlated with increased apoptosis, oxidative stress (elevated H2O2 and NO levels), and modulation of the Bax/Bcl-2 ratio, reinforcing the potential of SQD as a potent radiosensitizer in breast cancer therapy [40].

Despite the promising potential of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs in radiotherapy, several challenges remain before their clinical translation [51]. One of the primary concerns is biosafety and toxicity [34,97,98]. While QDs have demonstrated significant radiosensitization effects, their long-term effects on healthy tissues and overall systemic toxicity remain unclear [37]. Issues such as oxidative stress-induced DNA damage, potential mutagenicity, and immune system responses need comprehensive investigation [51].

Another major challenge is synthesis complexity and reproducibility [74]. The fabrication of QDs with precise size, surface modifications, and functionalization for optimal therapeutic efficacy is complex and requires stringent quality control [35]. Scalability remains a concern, as batch-to-batch variations could affect the consistency of therapeutic outcomes [52].

Targeting specificity is another issue in RT applications [40]. While QDs can accumulate in tumors through the enhanced EPR effect, off-target accumulation in vital organs, such as the liver and kidneys, raises safety concerns [34,51]. Functionalization strategies, such as ligand conjugation, require further refinement to ensure selective tumor targeting while minimizing systemic toxicity [51,74].

Biodegradability and clearance mechanisms are also crucial considerations [55]. Carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs need to be biodegradable and efficiently cleared from the body to prevent long-term accumulation and associated toxicities [35,37]. Although phosphorus-based QDs have shown some promise due to their degradability, further studies are required to optimize their clearance profiles [34].

Finally, regulatory and translational hurdles pose significant barriers to clinical implementation [52]. Given that QDs are a relatively new class of materials in oncology, extensive preclinical and clinical studies are needed to establish standardized safety protocols and obtain regulatory approvals for human use [74].

The future of carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs in radiotherapy holds great promise, provided that the existing challenges are systematically addressed [74]. One key area of development is engineering biocompatible and biodegradable QDs with minimal toxicity [37]. Surface modifications using biopolymers, peptides, or targeted ligands could improve their biosafety profile and enhance tumor specificity [74].

Emerging research in diverse medical fields, including the study of gut microbiota in peritoneal fibrosis and novel neurosurgical techniques like neuroendoscopic evacuation for intracerebral hemorrhage, highlights the multidisciplinary nature of modern therapeutic advancements. These studies underscore the importance of personalized and precise approaches in medicine, which align with the evolving potential of nanotechnology-based therapies in oncology [99–101]. Personalized nanomedicine approaches could revolutionize QD-based RT by tailoring nanoplatforms based on patient-specific tumor characteristics [36,74]. Advances in AI-driven drug delivery and imaging analytics may optimize the use of QDs for individualized cancer treatment [36,55].

Another exciting avenue is the integration of multi-functional QD-based systems [72,74]. Combining QDs with immunotherapy, gene therapy, or CRISPR-based genome editing could amplify their therapeutic efficacy [33,34]. Additionally, exploring the potential of QDs in photodynamic therapy alongside RT could offer synergistic treatment strategies [34].

Smart delivery systems and real-time monitoring will be critical for the future of QD-based RT [74]. Stimuli-responsive QDs that release therapeutic agents or enhance radiosensitization in response to tumor microenvironmental cues (such as pH, hypoxia, or radiation exposure) could optimize treatment precision while minimizing side effects [51,55].

Lastly, standardization and regulatory advancements will be essential for bringing QD-based RT to clinical settings [33,55]. Collaborative efforts between researchers, clinicians, and regulatory bodies will be necessary to establish guidelines for preclinical testing, clinical trials, and eventual FDA or EMA approval [51,72].

In conclusion, while carbon- and phosphorus-based QDs present a transformative opportunity for enhancing radiotherapy, overcoming current challenges will be key to their successful clinical translation [37,51]. With continued interdisciplinary research and innovation, these nanosystems could play a crucial role in next-generation cancer therapy [33,35].

CQDs and BPQDs have shown significant promise as effective radiosensitizers, enhancing both the precision and effectiveness of radiotherapy. These non-metallic quantum dots possess unique properties, such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, and the ability to generate ROS, all of which contribute to improved treatment outcomes while minimizing collateral damage to healthy tissues. By incorporating QDs into hybrid systems—combining them with therapeutic agents like bacteria, chemotherapeutics, and nanoparticles—the radiosensitization effect can be enhanced, enabling more targeted and efficient cancer treatments. However, despite these promising advancements, several challenges remain before clinical application, including concerns about biosafety, optimization of targeting specificity, and overcoming regulatory barriers. Research into biodegradability, surface functionalization, and precise tumor targeting will be crucial for developing safer and more effective therapies. Overall, the integration of CQDs and BPQDs into radiotherapy represents a promising step forward in cancer treatment, with future developments potentially revolutionizing current protocols and improving patient outcomes.
